diagrams.title: User-Operating System Interaction Architecture
diagrams.description: A layered architecture diagram showing how users interact with the operating system through various interface types, progressing from user applications down to hardware.
elements: Top Layer: User Applications (Web browser, text editor, games)
Second Layer: User Interface (CLI Terminal, GUI Desktop, Touch Interface)
Third Layer: System Call Interface (API functions, system call wrapper)
Fourth Layer: Operating System Kernel (Process management, Memory management, File system, Device drivers)
Bottom Layer: Hardware (CPU, RAM, Disk, I/O devices)
Arrows showing downward request flow and upward response flow
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the complete path of user interaction with hardware, learning how requests travel from user level through OS services to hardware and back. This clarifies the abstraction layers in OS design.

diagrams.title: Classification of User Interfaces
diagrams.description: A hierarchical diagram showing the different types of user interfaces available in operating systems.
diagrams.purpose: Students learn to classify different interface types and understand their hierarchical organization, which helps in choosing appropriate interfaces for different use cases.

diagrams.title: System Call Interface Flow Diagram
diagrams.description: A flowchart showing the step-by-step process of how a user command is executed through system calls to access OS services.
elements: Start: User enters command via Interface (CLI command or GUI click)
Process 2: Library function executes trap instruction (kernel mode switch)
Process 3: OS kernel receives system call number and parameters
Process 4: Kernel verifies parameters and permissions
Process 5: Kernel executes requested service (file open, process create, memory allocation)
Process 6: Kernel returns result/status to user space
End: Application receives response and displays output
Decision diamond for error checking at each step
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the mechanism of how user requests transition to kernel mode through system calls, learning about the privilege separation and security aspects of OS interfaces.

diagrams.title: Operating System Services and Interface Relationship
diagrams.description: A block diagram showing how OS services connect to user interfaces and what services each interface type provides access to.
elements: Left Side: CLI Connection (Text commands, Script execution)
Right Side: GUI Connection (Windows, Icons, Menus, Pointers)
Bottom: User Applications accessing services through interfaces
Arrows showing which services are accessible through each interface type
diagrams.purpose: Students understand how different user interfaces provide access to various OS services, learning about the relationship between interface types and system capabilities.

charts.title: CLI vs GUI vs Touch Interface Comparison
charts.content: CLI (Command-Line Interface): Text-based input/output; High speed for experts; Low resource usage; Requires command knowledge; Examples: Bash, PowerShell, Terminal. GUI (Graphical User Interface): Visual icons and windows; User-friendly; Higher resource usage; Intuitive learning curve; Examples: Windows, macOS, GNOME. Touch Interface: Gesture-based interaction; Mobile-optimized; Direct manipulation; Limited precision; Examples: iOS, Android.

charts.title: Types of System Calls
charts.content: Process Control: fork, exec, wait, exit, terminate. File Management: open, read, write, close, create, delete. Device Management: read, write, request, release. Information Maintenance: getpid, alarm, sleep, getuid. Communication: pipe, socket, send, receive, connect. Protection: chmod, chown, getuid, setuid.

charts.title: OS Services Provided to Users Through Interfaces
charts.content: Service | CLI Access | GUI Access | Purpose. Process Management | ps, kill, top | Task Manager | Control running programs. File Management | ls, cp, mv, rm | File Explorer | Organize and manipulate files. Memory Management | free, vmstat | System Monitor | View RAM usage. Device Management | mount, umount | Device Manager | Connect/disconnect hardware. Network Configuration | ifconfig, ping | Network Settings | Configure network connections. User Management | useradd, passwd | User Accounts | Create and manage users.

charts.title: User Request Processing Flow
charts.content: User Input → Interface Layer (CLI/GUI) → Parameter Validation → System Call Invocation → Kernel Mode Transition → Service Execution → Status Return → User Mode Transition → Result Display → User receives output. Error Path: Parameter Validation Failure → Error Code Return → Error Message Display.

mindMaps.purpose: Provides a comprehensive overview connecting all aspects of user-OS interaction, helping students see the relationships between interface types, OS services, and the underlying mechanisms that enable user-OS communication.

Breaks down the internal components that make up the user-OS interface, helping students understand the software architecture behind interface types and how different components work together to provide seamless interaction.
